answer:
In the *Gaṇapatitattva*, an Old Javanese (Kawi) *tattva* treatise attributed to Gaṇapati, "Jñāna" appears transliterated (capitalized as a proper divine name/entity) rather than rendered into a vernacular equivalent like "knowledge." It is one of three somatic divinities—alongside Śiva and Pramāṇa—whose locations in the body are mapped for yogic/liberative purposes.[^1]

[^1]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-ad9122).

The immediate context describes ritual unification of these divinities in the face of death omens:

> The Divine Śiva resides in the eyes; his color is yellow like polished gold. He is the Self of sight. The Divine Pramāṇa resides at the tip of the nose and at the tip of the tongue; his color is blue. He is the Self of speech. **The Divine Jñāna resides at the base of the lungs**; his color is white without stain. He is the divine Self of memory. Furthermore, if there is a sign of death, unite The Divine Śiva with The Divine Pramāṇa and **The Divine Jñāna**. Such is the path of The Divine Śiva.[^1]

This usage aligns closely with Sanskrit tantric Śaiva (and Śākta) frameworks, such as those in the Āgamas or Tantras (e.g., *Mālinīvijayottara* or *Kubjikāmata*), where *jñāna* is hypostatized as a deity or *śakti* embodying discriminative wisdom/gnosis (*vidyā*), often paired with *pramāṇa* (valid cognition) and *śiva* (consciousness). Here, Jñāna's white color evokes purity/clarity (like Sanskrit *suddha vidyā*), its lung-location links to *smṛti* (memory/retention of gnosis), and the death-rite unification recalls Sanskrit *marṇa-saṃskāra* practices for merging *jñāna-śakti* into Śiva at dissolution—no evident semantic shift, but a direct doctrinal transplant into a Javanese Śaiva yogic schema.[^2]

[^2]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, work-level overview, [wiki card](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/wiki/_work.card.md); ch.1 wiki, [card](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/wiki/01.card.md). Doctrinal parallels inferred from corpus Sanskrit uses (e.g., *Manusmṛti* 12 on *ātmajñāna* as supreme gnosis[^3]; *Pārāśara Smṛti* 1 on *jñāna* as Treta-yuga pinnacle[^4]), but the somatic deification is distinctly tantric, matching Javanese Śaiva adaptations like *Vṛhaspatitattva*.

Parallel transliterated uses appear elsewhere in Kawi *tattva*-adjacent texts (e.g., *Kalpabuddha* attributes *jñāna* types to buddhas; *Saṃ Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan* expounds *adwayajñāna* as non-dual gnosis), consistently denoting salvific wisdom without translation.[^5]

[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/#p-074f6e).
[^4]: *Pārāśara Smṛti*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-afd7d9).
[^5]: E.g., *Kalpabuddha* 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-kalpabuddha-d760dc/01/translation/#p-e1c8df); *Saṃ Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan* 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19/01/translation/#p-71f1a3).
